entry.title: Hola Browser Cryptominer, Stripe Magecart Abuse, and DentaQuest Data Leak Lead Digest
entry.content: This coverage window is dominated by supply chain and web-commerce threats, with a compromised Windows browser installer delivering cryptomining malware and a Magecart campaign abusing trusted cloud and payment infrastructure. Large-scale data exposure and cyber policy developments also feature prominently, alongside continued industry focus on AI-enabled threat hunting and exposure management. Hola confirmed that the Windows version of Hola Browser was compromised in a supply chain incident that delivered an undeclared executable identified by researchers as a cryptocurrency miner. Sophos observed the file, named me.exe, under C:\Program Files\Hola\, with behavior including Windows Defender exclusion creation, persistence as HolaMonitorService.exe, an auto-starting service named holamonitorsvc, and execution when the host is idle. Hola said roughly 0.1% of users were affected and reported no evidence of user data access, theft, or compromise. Sansec reported a Magecart campaign targeting Magento and Adobe Commerce checkout pages that abuses Google Tag Manager and Stripe infrastructure. The skimmer retrieves JavaScript from metadata fields in a Stripe customer record, reassembles and executes it, then captures payment card details, names, billing and email addresses, and phone numbers. Stolen data is XOR-obfuscated, stored locally, and later uploaded by creating fake Stripe customer records containing the data in metadata fields; Sansec also observed a variant using a Google Firestore document named tracking/captcha in a project called braintree-payment-app. Browser trust and software distribution were also in focus with Brave Software’s release of Brave Origin, a paid minimalist browser build that removes cryptocurrency, AI, rewards, VPN promotions, sponsored content, and other monetization features while retaining Brave Shields. The release drew mixed user reaction, with some criticizing the paid model and others noting similar reductions can be achieved in the free browser through enterprise policies. DentaQuest confirmed unauthorized access to a limited portion of its network after the ShinyHunters extortion group claimed to have stolen more than 234 GB of company data and later publicly leaked it. Have I Been Pwned analyzed the dataset and found records for 2.6 million accounts, including email addresses, full names, phone numbers, government-issued IDs, health insurance information, genders, and dates of birth. DentaQuest said its systems remain operational and that external experts were engaged to investigate the incident and determine the compromised data. The Federal Trade Commission is considering whether to modify or set aside a 2022 settlement order with Twitter, now X, related to the use of account security data for targeted advertising. The original order included a $150 million penalty and restrictions on profiting from deceptively collected phone numbers and email addresses. X argued that the order is unfair because it was issued against a company that no longer exists, responsible employees have departed, and the company has established a privacy and data protection program; public comments are open until July 2. The Trump administration is reportedly considering Palantir Technologies CTO Shyam Sankar to lead CISA, although a White House official disputed the report after publication. CISA has lacked a Senate-confirmed director since Jen Easterly stepped down in January 2025, with Acting Director Nick Andersen leading the agency since February. The agency is also named in the administration’s artificial intelligence executive order as a key implementation body. Russia’s Supreme Court is set to consider designating Belarusian Cyber Partisans and Silent Crow as extremist organizations in a closed-door hearing. The groups have claimed cyberattacks against Russian and Belarusian government and critical infrastructure targets, including a July 2025 attack on Aeroflot that reportedly forced more than 100 flight cancellations and disrupted roughly 20,000 passengers. The hackers claimed they destroyed Aeroflot IT infrastructure and exfiltrated sensitive operational and employee-related data. Tenable announced participation in Anthropic’s Project Glasswing, where it is evaluating Claude Mythos Preview for exposure management use cases. The company said it will assess frontier AI reasoning for attack path analysis, exposure prioritization, remediation decision-making, and strengthening Tenable’s own security operations. Tenable also framed AI systems that organizations did not build as an expanding area requiring visibility and governance. Cisco Talos described an expansion of its Threat Hunting program that combines AI-driven telemetry analysis with human validation across endpoint, network, and identity data. Talos said its hypothesis-driven approach helped identify KongTuke command-and-control discovery activity before a formal detection signature existed. Its reporting from Cisco Live also highlighted AI as a major theme across infrastructure, security, data movement, and agentic computing environments.
entry.summary: This coverage window is dominated by supply chain and web-commerce threats, with a compromised Windows browser installer delivering cryptomining malware and a Magecart campaign abusing trusted cloud and payment infrastructure. Large-scale data exposure and cyber policy developments also feature...

entry.title: Cisco CVE-2026-20230, IronWorm npm Malware, WFP Gaza Breach, and AI Workflow Risks
entry.content: This coverage window was dominated by high-impact vulnerability exposure, software supply-chain compromise, and data protection failures affecting both enterprise and humanitarian environments. Public exploit code for Cisco Unified CM, a fast-moving npm malware campaign, and a major World Food Programme breach all point to continued pressure on externally reachable systems, developer ecosystems, and sensitive identity datasets. AI remained a cross-cutting theme, with new reporting on agentic workflow abuse, federal AI security governance, and operational risks tied to model access and automation. Cisco patched CVE-2026-20230 in Unified Communications Manager and Unified CM Session Management Edition, a server-side request forgery flaw with public proof-of-concept exploit code available. The vulnerability can allow an unauthenticated network attacker to write arbitrary files to the underlying operating system, with Cisco noting that follow-on escalation to root is possible even though the CVSS score reflects only the file-write impact. The issue is exploitable when the Cisco WebDialer service is running, which is disabled by default. The vulnerability appeared again in broader threat reporting noting no known active exploitation at publication time. Separate research into underground discussion showed how threat actors are operationalizing newly disclosed vulnerabilities: a forum user named “Hercules” described workflows for finding disclosures, locating exposed systems, validating likely vulnerable targets, and choosing between reporting, selling, or exploiting access. The tutorial’s focus on Nuclei and monetization paths reflects how vulnerability intelligence can quickly move from disclosure into opportunistic targeting. A new npm supply-chain attack infected 36 packages with IronWorm, a Rust-based infostealer targeting 86 environment variables and 20 credential files associated with services including OpenAI, AWS, Anthropic, npm, Vault, SSH, and Exodus wallets. JFrog reported that IronWorm hides behind an eBPF kernel rootkit, communicates over Tor, and self-propagates using stolen npm publishing credentials, including secrets tied to npm Trusted Publishing. The campaign reportedly began from a compromised npm account named “asteroiddao,” which published malicious package versions using a preinstall execution path. Developer automation also featured in reporting on a flaw in Anthropic’s Claude Code GitHub Action. The issue allowed an attacker to compromise vulnerable public repositories by opening a single GitHub issue, because the workflow trusted actors whose names ended in “[bot].” Researcher RyotaK used indirect prompt injection to make Claude expose environment variables, including credentials used by GitHub Actions to request an OIDC token; Anthropic fixed the core issue after the January report and included fixes in claude-code-action v1.0.94. AI-related risk reporting continued to expand from model safety into infrastructure, access control, and autonomous workflow behavior. One article argued that agentic AI adoption in defense environments depends on secure infrastructure, governance, and controls around what data enters models, who or what accesses them, and how agents reach across databases, mission systems, and coalition environments. The discussion emphasized classified networks, cross-domain data movement, and policy enforcement around sensitive data before it reaches AI systems. CISA is expected to issue a binding operational directive tied to the president’s artificial intelligence executive order, with Acting Director Nick Andersen saying it will focus partly on vulnerability alleviation and vulnerability management. Andersen also said CISA will help establish the cyber clearinghouse envisioned by the executive order and will gain access to models for vetting. In parallel, a threat bulletin described AI tools such as Cursor and Anthropic Claude Opus being used by an unknown actor to automate Active Directory discovery and refine EDR evasion in a post-exploitation framework, while Cyera identified 344 verified enterprise-relevant agent-inflicted damage cases among publicly reported AI incidents. The United Nations World Food Programme disclosed a breach of its self-registration application for Palestine, used for assistance registration in Gaza. Attackers accessed personal data including names, ID numbers, phone numbers, and location-related registration information such as neighborhood data. The WFP told The New Humanitarian that the May 14 breach involved data belonging to people in roughly 600,000 Palestinian households in Gaza, and the platform was temporarily suspended while the organization investigated and implemented security improvements. A separate privacy enforcement development came from the U.S. Supreme Court, which ruled 8-1 that the FCC acted legally when issuing nearly $200 million in fines against telecommunications companies accused of sharing access to consumer location data without consent. Verizon, AT&T, T-Mobile, and Sprint were implicated in enforcement actions tied to allegations that they failed to take reasonable measures to protect location information and attempted to shift consent obligations downstream. The FCC’s actions followed Sen. Ron Wyden’s investigation into a contractor that bought the data and enabled law enforcement access to phone location information without a court order. The ThreatsDay bulletin highlighted DriveSurge, a malware distribution operation active since September 2025 that uses ClickFix and FakeUpdates lures on compromised websites through the zTDS traffic distribution system. The same bulletin noted that nation-state groups including APT29, APT33, and UTA0355 have used the open-source ROADtools framework in cloud intrusions. Their use of legitimate Microsoft APIs for discovery, persistence, and defense evasion illustrates continued abuse of administrative and research tooling inside cloud environments.
entry.summary: This coverage window was dominated by high-impact vulnerability exposure, software supply-chain compromise, and data protection failures affecting both enterprise and humanitarian environments. Public exploit code for Cisco Unified CM, a fast-moving npm malware campaign, and a major World Food...

entry.title: WFP Gaza Data Breach, TA4922 Phishing, FlutterShell, and Cisco CVE-2026-20230
entry.content: This digest window highlights a mix of data exposure, espionage-linked recruitment, financially motivated phishing, macOS malvertising, and critical enterprise infrastructure vulnerability reporting. Several items center on identity and access: exposed humanitarian recipient data, GitHub token theft via developer tooling, job-platform recruitment for insider access, and phishing workflows designed to move targets outside monitored channels. The United Nations World Food Programme is investigating a breach affecting its Gaza self-registration application for food and cash assistance. The exposed data reportedly included names, identification numbers, phone numbers, and location details, including neighborhood information, with The New Humanitarian citing a WFP spokesperson saying sensitive information for about 600,000 Palestinian households was exposed. WFP temporarily suspended the platform while investigating and said it had contained the intrusion and strengthened controls. European law enforcement also disrupted an identity-fraud marketplace allegedly supporting migrant smuggling within the European Union. French and Spanish authorities arrested a suspect in Alicante, Spain, and seized production equipment and roughly 800 counterfeit European identity documents. Europol characterized document fraud as a major enabler of fraudulent residency legalization and migrant smuggling in its 2025 serious organized crime threat assessment. The Five Eyes intelligence partnership warned that Chinese military intelligence services are using online job platforms and recruiter personas to identify people with access to sensitive information. The bulletin described targeting of clearance holders, military personnel, academics, journalists, and think tank employees, with applicants allegedly ranked by likely access and asked to produce trial reports on strategic topics. China’s Ministry of Foreign Affairs denied the allegations and accused Five Eyes governments of conducting their own global espionage activities. Proofpoint reported that the Chinese-speaking actor TA4922 has expanded phishing activity to organizations in the U.K., Germany, Italy, and South Africa. The campaigns use HR, business, tax, invoice, benefits, and compliance lures for credential phishing, fraud, and malware delivery. Reported tooling includes ValleyRAT, Atlas RAT, RomulusLoader, and SilentRunLoader. Proofpoint assesses TA4922 as likely financially motivated, with objectives including data theft, fraud, access resale, and persistent remote access. The actor has also attempted to shift conversations from email into out-of-band channels such as LINE, WhatsApp, and Microsoft Teams, a tactic that can reduce visibility for enterprise email security controls while maintaining social-engineering continuity. Security researcher Ammar Askar published a working proof-of-concept exploit for a VS Code issue that could steal a victim’s GitHub access token after a single link click. Askar said he bypassed Microsoft’s official reporting process because of dissatisfaction with how Microsoft handled a previous report, including allegedly fixing it silently while denying security impact. The release follows recent reporting on TeamPCP compromising thousands of GitHub internal repositories through a poisoned VS Code extension and comes amid broader controversy over uncoordinated Windows zero-day disclosures. Microsoft separately attributed unexpected driver and BIOS updates on some Windows systems to a misconfiguration in the Windows Update caching service. The issue temporarily dropped device enrollment information, causing some devices to be treated as non-enrolled despite policies intended to prevent automatic updates. Microsoft said the installed drivers were approved and signed and did not pose a security threat, though administrators reported operational impact including broken audio or video devices. Palo Alto Networks Unit 42 reported Operation FlutterBridge, a macOS malvertising campaign distributing a new Flutter-based backdoor named FlutterShell. The campaign uses malicious Google and YouTube ads from Google-verified shell companies to lure users in the U.S., Canada, Australia, France, and Germany into installing malware disguised as legitimate desktop applications. FlutterShell includes adware behavior and backdoor capabilities such as shell command execution, file system manipulation, and environment variable exfiltration, with a WebView architecture that lets attackers alter hosted malicious logic without recompiling the binary. Cisco disclosed CVE-2026-20230, a critical Cisco Unified Communications Manager vulnerability that can allow remote, unauthenticated attackers to gain root privileges through low-complexity SSRF attacks. Cisco PSIRT said public proof-of-concept exploit code exists, but it has not observed active exploitation or targeting. The vulnerability affects systems where the WebDialer service is enabled, which Cisco said is disabled by default. Cisco Talos also published two pieces on its threat-hunting model and leadership perspective, emphasizing hypothesis-driven hunting across endpoint, firewall, and identity telemetry. A KongTuke case study described correlating outbound firewall connections to 144.31.221.82 on port 6060 with endpoint evidence of encoded PowerShell, Invoke-WebRequest, curl.exe activity, and cleanup via Remove-Item. Talos framed AI as accelerating both attacker and defender capabilities while preserving human analyst judgment for validation and environmental context.
entry.summary: This digest window highlights a mix of data exposure, espionage-linked recruitment, financially motivated phishing, macOS malvertising, and critical enterprise infrastructure vulnerability reporting. Several items center on identity and access: exposed humanitarian recipient data, GitHub token...

entry.title: Magento CVE-2026-45247 Exploitation, Fake OSS Malware Sites, and Stock Exchange Espionage
entry.content: Reporting in this coverage window highlighted a mix of financially motivated malware delivery, active exploitation of e-commerce infrastructure, targeted espionage against financial-sector leadership, and law enforcement disruption of large-scale crypto fraud ecosystems. The most operationally significant items were the addition of Magento extension flaw CVE-2026-45247 to CISA’s KEV catalog and a search-driven malware distribution campaign abusing fake open-source tooling sites. Researchers reported a large-scale malware delivery operation using fake websites that impersonate open-source and freeware projects, including Ghidra, dnSpy, and SpiderFoot. Some of the impersonation sites ranked highly in Google search results, increasing the likelihood that users searching for legitimate security or developer tools would encounter malicious download pages. The sites routed visitors through a Traffic Distribution System with a CloudFront-hosted JavaScript staging layer and gated redirect logic. The chain included first-visit checks, click confirmation, anti-bot controls, VPN and datacenter filtering, and frequency capping before delivering payloads such as SessionGate, Remus Stealer, and AnimateClipper. Check Point reported that infrastructure previously associated with traffic generation and advertising was repurposed for malware distribution starting in January 2026, while VirusTotal telemetry showed roughly 2,000 to 3,500 submissions tied to SessionGate samples. In parallel, CISA added CVE-2026-45247 to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog following reports of active exploitation against the Mirasvit Cache Warmer full-page cache extension for Magento. The flaw is a deserialization of untrusted data issue affecting versions before 1.11.12 and can allow unauthenticated attackers to execute arbitrary PHP code via crafted serialized PHP objects in the CacheWarmer cookie. Imperva observed exploitation attempts using base64-encoded serialized PHP object payloads in malicious HTTP requests. Symantec and Carbon Black’s Threat Hunter Team described an intrusion in which unknown attackers maintained access to the Outlook mailbox of a senior executive at a major global stock exchange for at least five months. The activity was characterized as intelligence collection rather than financially motivated theft, with no public attribution to a specific threat group. The attackers used a mailbox stealer built on the legitimate Aspose .NET library to convert Outlook OST and PST data into PST files and export messages in date-based batches. Exfiltration was routed through Dropbox and OneDrive Personal, and the tooling included connections to hard-coded Microsoft IP addresses to avoid DNS lookups for onedrive.live.com. Symantec also reported related indicators including FRPC, Secretsdump, SharpDecryptPwd, and a Windows User Account Control bypass tool. The U.S. Department of Justice announced a “Disruption Week” operation that began on May 18, 2026, targeting cyber-enabled and cryptocurrency fraud networks defrauding Americans. The operation disrupted or took down more than 1.4 million Facebook and Instagram accounts, pages, and groups, along with 20,000 Microsoft accounts and thousands of Starlink kits. Private-sector entities voluntarily froze more than $3.8 million in cryptocurrency linked to laundering funds stolen from victims. The effort involved companies including Apple, Coinbase, Google, Meta, Microsoft, Silent Push, SpaceX/Starlink, TRM Labs, and Zenlayer, alongside law enforcement partners from the United States, Australia, Canada, New Zealand, Thailand, and the United Kingdom. The DoJ linked many cryptocurrency investment scam operations to industrial-scale compounds in Cambodia, Laos, and Burma along the Thai border. The announcement emphasized that some workers in these compounds may be trafficking victims forced to conduct fraud activity, underscoring the overlap between cyber-enabled financial crime, cryptocurrency laundering, and transnational organized exploitation.
entry.summary: Reporting in this coverage window highlighted a mix of financially motivated malware delivery, active exploitation of e-commerce infrastructure, targeted espionage against financial-sector leadership, and law enforcement disruption of large-scale crypto fraud ecosystems. The most operationally...

entry.title: Self-Replicating binding.gyp npm Supply Chain Worm Targets Developer Credentials
entry.content: This digest is dominated by a single but high-impact software supply chain report: an active self-replicating attack spreading through the npm ecosystem. The campaign abuses a less common execution path in Node.js package installation, using binding.gyp and node-gyp rather than relying on the more widely monitored preinstall or postinstall lifecycle hooks. StepSecurity reported an active npm supply chain attack that behaves like a worm by stealing developer and cloud credentials, then using those credentials to compromise additional packages and repositories. The attacker injects a malicious binding.gyp file into packages, causing node-gyp to execute code silently during npm install. This technique is notable because many supply chain detections focus on package lifecycle scripts, while binding.gyp provides another installation-time execution mechanism. The malware’s credential harvesting scope is broad and aimed at both developer workstations and build environments. Reported targets include npm, GitHub, AWS, Google Cloud Platform, Microsoft Azure, HashiCorp Vault, Kubernetes, RubyGems, and password manager tooling such as 1Password CLI, gopass, and pass. The targeting indicates an intent to collect secrets that can enable both immediate package publication abuse and deeper access into cloud, source control, and infrastructure management environments. A central propagation mechanism involves stolen GitHub tokens. According to the report, the worm uses those tokens to modify GitHub Actions workflow files so the malicious payload executes in future CI jobs associated with affected repositories. This creates persistence inside development pipelines and expands the blast radius beyond the originally infected package installation. The campaign also uses stolen npm and RubyGems tokens to publish poisoned versions of packages maintained by victims. That behavior allows the attack to spread through downstream dependency chains as users and automated systems install newly compromised package versions. The combination of registry credential theft, CI workflow modification, and cross-ecosystem package poisoning makes the activity especially relevant to threat hunters, detection engineers, SOC analysts, and incident responders tracking software supply chain compromise.
entry.summary: This digest is dominated by a single but high-impact software supply chain report: an active self-replicating attack spreading through the npm ecosystem. The campaign abuses a less common execution path in Node.js package installation, using binding.gyp and node-gyp rather than relying on the more...

entry.title: Cisco CVE-2026-20230, IronWorm npm Malware, Stripe Magecart, and WFP Gaza Breach
entry.content:
This 24-hour digest is dominated by supply-chain compromise, exposed payment and personal data, public exploit availability, and the continued operationalization of AI across both attack and defense. Several reports also show attackers abusing trusted platforms—Stripe, Google Tag Manager, npm, GitHub Actions, job sites, and browser update channels—to blend malicious activity into normal workflows. Hola confirmed a supply-chain compromise affecting the Windows version of Hola Browser, where some installations received an undeclared executable, me.exe, assessed by researchers as a Monero miner. The malware added a Windows Defender exclusion, copied itself as HolaMonitorService.exe, created the holamonitorsvc auto-start service, and ran during idle periods. Hola said roughly 0.1% of users were affected and reported no evidence of user data theft. The npm ecosystem saw another serious software supply-chain incident with IronWorm, a Rust-based infostealer found in 36 packages. JFrog reported that IronWorm targeted 86 environment variables and 20 credential files, used Tor communications, hid behind an eBPF kernel rootkit, and attempted self-propagation using stolen npm publishing credentials. The attack reportedly began from a compromised npm account named asteroiddao and was detected before it spread into more popular packages. Sansec reported a Magecart campaign targeting Magento and Adobe Commerce checkout pages by abusing Google Tag Manager and Stripe infrastructure. The skimmer retrieved JavaScript from Stripe customer metadata, executed it dynamically, captured payment and billing details, and later exfiltrated stolen data by creating fake Stripe customer records. A related variant used a Google Firestore document named tracking/captcha in a project called braintree-payment-app. Cisco patched CVE-2026-20230 in Unified Communications Manager and Unified CM Session Management Edition after public proof-of-concept exploit code became available. The vulnerability is an unauthenticated SSRF that can allow arbitrary file writes to the underlying operating system, with potential follow-on root escalation. Cisco noted the flaw is exploitable only when WebDialer is running, and WebDialer is disabled by default. A flaw in Anthropic’s Claude Code GitHub Action allowed a malicious GitHub issue to hijack vulnerable public repositories running the action. The issue stemmed from trusting actors whose names ended in [bot], combined with indirect prompt injection that caused Claude to expose environment variables, including credentials used to request OIDC tokens. Anthropic fixed the core issue after a January report and included hardened behavior in claude-code-action v1.0.94. Separate reporting highlighted growing friction around vulnerability disclosure after researcher Ammar Askar published a working exploit for a VS Code issue that could steal a victim’s GitHub token via a single link click. BleepingComputer also covered a threat actor tutorial explaining how attackers search for newly disclosed vulnerabilities, validate exposed systems, and choose between reporting, selling, or exploiting findings. The UN World Food Programme disclosed a breach of its Palestine self-registration application used for food and cash assistance in Gaza. The exposed data included names, ID numbers, phone numbers, and location details such as neighborhood information, affecting roughly 600,000 Palestinian households according to statements cited by The New Humanitarian. WFP temporarily suspended the platform while investigating and strengthening controls. DentaQuest confirmed unauthorized access to a limited portion of its network after ShinyHunters claimed to have stolen more than 234 GB of data and later leaked it. Have I Been Pwned analyzed the leak and found records for 2.6 million accounts, including names, email addresses, phone numbers, government-issued IDs, health insurance information, genders, and dates of birth. In the regulatory sphere, the U.S. Supreme Court upheld FCC fines against telecom carriers for sharing access to consumers’ location data without consent. Separately, the FTC opened public comment on whether to modify or set aside a 2022 order and $150 million penalty against Twitter, now X, over the use of account security data for targeted advertising. The Five Eyes intelligence partnership warned that Chinese military intelligence services are using online job platforms and front-company recruiter personas to identify people with access to sensitive or classified information. The bulletin said candidates may be asked about government contacts, military roles, or unit activities and later paid to produce reports on strategic topics. Proofpoint reported that Chinese-speaking threat actor TA4922 expanded phishing activity into the U.K., Germany, Italy, and South Africa. The group uses HR, tax, invoice, benefits, and compliance lures for credential phishing, fraud, and malware delivery, including ValleyRAT, Atlas RAT, RomulusLoader, and SilentRunLoader. Proofpoint assesses the actor as likely financially motivated. Russia’s Supreme Court is set to consider designating Belarusian Cyber Partisans and Silent Crow as extremist organizations. Both groups have claimed disruptive cyber operations against Russian and Belarusian government and critical infrastructure targets, including a July 2025 Aeroflot incident that reportedly disrupted more than 100 flights. AI remained a recurring theme across defensive operations and policy. CISA Acting Director Nick Andersen said the agency plans to release a binding operational directive tied to the administration’s AI executive order, with emphasis on vulnerability management and model vetting through a cyber clearinghouse. The administration is also reportedly close to nominating a Senate-confirmed CISA director, though reporting that Palantir CTO Shyam Sankar was under consideration was disputed by a White House official. Cisco Talos described expansion of its threat hunting program across endpoint, network, firewall, and identity telemetry, combining AI-driven analysis with human validation. Tenable also announced participation in Anthropic’s Project Glasswing to evaluate Claude Mythos Preview for exposure management, attack path analysis, remediation decision-making, and AI governance.

Cisco Patches CVE-2026-20230 in Unified CM as Exploit Code Goes Public — Must Read

Cisco patched a critical Unified Communications Manager flaw, CVE-2026-20230, that lets unauthenticated network attackers write files and potentially escalate to root when WebDialer is enabled. Proof-of-concept code is already public, so affected deployments should patch or disable WebDialer immediately.

  • Cisco patched CVE-2026-20230 in Unified Communications Manager and Unified CM Session Management Edition, and public proof-of-concept exploit code is already available.
  • The flaw is a server-side request forgery that can allow an unauthenticated network attacker to write arbitrary files to the underlying operating system and later escalate to root.
  • Cisco rated the advisory Critical even though the CVSS base score is 8.6 because the score covers the file-write impact but not the follow-on root escalation.
  • The vulnerability is exploitable only when the Cisco WebDialer service is running, and WebDialer is disabled by default.
  • Cisco says the fix is 14SU6 for the 14 train, while 15-train users must use an interim COP patch or disable WebDialer until 15SU5 is released.

New IronWorm malware hits 36 packages in npm supply-chain attack — Should Read

A new npm supply-chain attack infected 36 packages with IronWorm, a Rust-based infostealer that targets developer and CI secrets and can self-propagate by using stolen npm publishing credentials. Researchers say it was caught early before spreading to more popular packages, but affected developers should update packages, rotate credentials, and enable 2FA.

  • A new npm supply-chain attack infected 36 packages with IronWorm, an infostealer targeting 86 environment variables and 20 credential files that may contain OpenAI, AWS, Anthropic, npm, vault, SSH, and Exodus wallet credentials.
  • JFrog researchers said IronWorm is written in Rust, hides behind an eBPF kernel rootkit, communicates over the Tor network, and self-propagates by using stolen npm publishing credentials, including secrets tied to npm Trusted Publishing.
  • According to JFrog, the attack began from a compromised npm account named “asteroiddao,” which published package versions containing a Rust ELF binary executed via “preinstall” and pushed malicious commits into repositories.
  • JFrog found a GitHub Actions-based mechanism for exfiltrating serialized secrets as build artifacts, but said this delivery mechanism was not used in the analyzed IronWorm supply-chain attack.
  • Ox Security said the IronWorm attack was detected very early and stopped before it spread to more popular npm packages, and recommended developers upgrade to fixed releases, rotate keys, and enable two-factor authentication.

Claude Code GitHub Action Flaw Let One Malicious Issue Hijack Repositories — Should Read

A flaw in Anthropic’s Claude Code GitHub Action let a malicious GitHub issue from an attacker-controlled bot trigger Claude, exfiltrate workflow credentials via prompt injection, and potentially gain write access to vulnerable repositories. Anthropic patched the issue in v1.0.94, and users should update and restrict untrusted workflow triggers and permissions.

  • A flaw in Anthropic's Claude Code GitHub Action allowed an attacker to take over vulnerable public repositories running it by opening a single GitHub issue.
  • The trigger check trusted any actor whose name ended in “[bot],” even though anyone can create a GitHub App and use it to open issues or pull requests on public repositories.
  • RyotaK used indirect prompt injection to get Claude to expose environment variables, including credentials used by GitHub Actions to request an OIDC token.
  • Anthropic fixed the core issue within four days of the January report, continued hardening the action through the spring, and included the fixes in claude-code-action v1.0.94.
  • The article recommends updating to claude-code-action v1.0.94 or later and auditing workflows that allow users without write access or bots to trigger Claude.

Credit card theft campaign abuses Stripe to host stolen payment info — Should Read

A new Magecart credit card skimming campaign is abusing trusted Google Tag Manager and Stripe API infrastructure to load malicious code on checkout pages and store stolen payment data as fake Stripe customer records. Because the traffic goes through commonly allowed domains, the skimmer can bypass many content security and network filtering controls.

  • Sansec discovered a new Magecart campaign that uses Google Tag Manager to load malicious code and Stripe's API infrastructure to host both the credit card-stealing payload and exfiltrated data.
  • The skimmer targets Magento and Adobe Commerce checkout pages to capture payment card details, customer names, billing and email addresses, and phone numbers.
  • The malicious code retrieves JavaScript from metadata fields in a Stripe customer record, reassembles it, and executes it using new Function().
  • Stolen payment data is obfuscated with XOR, stored locally, and later uploaded by creating fake Stripe customer records with the data placed in metadata fields.
  • Sansec also found a variant that retrieves its payload from a Google Firestore document named tracking/captcha in a project called braintree-payment-app.

DentaQuest data breach exposed info of 2.6 million accounts — Should Read

DentaQuest confirmed unauthorized access to part of its network after ShinyHunters leaked stolen data, with Have I Been Pwned finding records for 2.6 million accounts exposed. The leaked information includes names, emails, phone numbers, government IDs, health insurance details, genders, and dates of birth, raising phishing and identity theft risks.

  • DentaQuest confirmed that its networks were breached through unauthorized access to a limited portion of its network.
  • The ShinyHunters extortion group claimed to have stolen more than 234 GB of DentaQuest data and later publicly leaked it after failing to reach an agreement with the company.
  • Have I Been Pwned analyzed the leaked dataset and found that it contained records for 2.6 million accounts.
  • The exposed data included email addresses, full names, phone numbers, government-issued IDs, health insurance information, genders, and dates of birth.
  • DentaQuest said its systems remain fully operational and that it engaged external experts to investigate the incident and determine what data was compromised.

UN food agency discloses breach affecting 600,000 Gaza households — Should Read

The UN World Food Programme says its Gaza self-registration platform was breached, exposing names, ID numbers, phone numbers, and location details for people in about 600,000 Palestinian households. WFP has temporarily suspended the platform, says aid will continue, and warned beneficiaries to watch for scams.

  • The United Nations World Food Programme disclosed that its self-registration application for Palestine, used for assistance registration in Gaza, was breached.
  • The attackers accessed personal data of Gaza beneficiaries, including names, ID numbers, phone numbers, and location information such as neighborhood data recorded during registration.
  • The WFP told The New Humanitarian that the attackers breached its systems on May 14 and stole information belonging to people in roughly 600,000 Palestinian households in Gaza.
  • The WFP temporarily suspended the self-registration platform to implement urgent security and system protection improvements while it investigates the incident.
  • The WFP warned Palestinian beneficiaries to be wary of anyone claiming to represent the organization and requesting information or money, and not to click suspicious links or messages.

China-Linked TA4922 Expands Phishing Attacks to UK, Germany, Italy, and South Africa — Should Read

China-linked TA4922 is rapidly expanding phishing operations beyond East Asia to targets in the U.K., Germany, Italy, South Africa, and Southeast Asia, using HR, business, tax, and invoice lures to deliver RATs and new loaders. Proofpoint assesses the group as financially motivated, seeking remote access for data theft, fraud, access resale, or persistent access.

  • Proofpoint is tracking a Chinese-speaking threat actor as TA4922, which has expanded its targeting to European organizations in the U.K., Germany, Italy, and South Africa.
  • TA4922 uses phishing campaigns with human resources-, business-, tax-, invoice-, benefits-, and compliance-themed lures for credential phishing, fraud, and malware delivery.
  • The group’s malware arsenal includes ValleyRAT, Atlas RAT, RomulusLoader, and SilentRunLoader.
  • Proofpoint assesses TA4922 as likely financially motivated and focused on obtaining remote access for financial gain, including data theft, fraud, access resale, or persistent access.
  • TA4922 has attempted to move conversations from email to out-of-band channels such as LINE, WhatsApp, and Microsoft Teams to bypass enterprise security controls and steal data or deliver malware.

Researcher publishes GitHub token-stealing exploit, blames Microsoft’s disclosure process — Should Read

A researcher publicly released a working exploit for a VS Code flaw that can steal GitHub access tokens via a single link click, citing frustration with Microsoft’s vulnerability disclosure process. The incident highlights growing tensions between Microsoft and security researchers, with uncoordinated exploit releases increasing risk before fixes are available.

  • Security researcher Ammar Askar published a working proof-of-concept exploit for a flaw in Microsoft VS Code that could allow an attacker to steal a victim’s GitHub access token with a single link click.
  • Askar said he bypassed Microsoft’s official reporting process because Microsoft’s Security Response Center had previously fixed an issue he reported without crediting him and while denying it had security impact.
  • The disclosure followed a recent breach of thousands of GitHub internal repositories by the cybercrime group TeamPCP through a poisoned VS Code extension.
  • Another researcher known as Nightmare Eclipse has recently published several Windows zero-days without coordinating with Microsoft, also citing grievances with Microsoft’s disclosure handling.
  • Microsoft recently clarified that it has no intention to pursue action against researchers who find and publish vulnerabilities and acknowledged that some interactions with researchers have fallen short.

Five Eyes warn Chinese spies are using job sites to recruit insiders — Should Read

Five Eyes intelligence agencies warned that Chinese military intelligence is using online job platforms and fake recruiter roles to target people with access to government, military, or policy information. Applicants may be steered into providing increasingly sensitive reports for payment, even when the information is not formally classified.

  • The Five Eyes intelligence partnership warned that China’s military intelligence services are using online job platforms with thousands of adverts to recruit people with access to sensitive information.
  • Chinese intelligence officers are allegedly posing as recruiters and consultants for front companies based outside China to target government and military personnel and others with access to classified or privileged information.
  • The bulletin says recruiters rank applicants’ résumés based on likely access to sensitive information and may ask candidates during virtual interviews about government contacts, military roles, or unit activities.
  • Successful candidates may be asked to produce reports on topics of strategic interest to China, with payments ranging from a few hundred to several thousand dollars per report.
  • China’s Ministry of Foreign Affairs denied the allegations, calling the claims fabricated and accusing the Five Eyes countries of engaging in global espionage.

Hola Browser for Windows compromised to deliver cryptominer — Should Read

Hola confirmed its Windows browser distribution pipeline was compromised in a supply-chain attack that installed an undeclared Monero cryptominer on a small share of users’ systems. The company says it rebuilt its pipeline and added stronger code-signing checks, access controls, and monitoring.

  • The Windows version of Hola Browser was compromised in a supply chain attack that delivered an undeclared executable identified by researchers as a cryptocurrency miner.
  • Sophos and other cybersecurity companies found an undeclared file named me.exe installed in some cases under C:\Program Files\Hola\ during AppEsteem certification checks.
  • Sophos found signs that me.exe was a Monero cryptocurrency miner, including strings pointing to its true nature.
  • The miner adds a Windows Defender exclusion rule, copies itself to Program Files as HolaMonitorService.exe, creates an auto-starting Windows service named hola_monitor_svc, and runs when the computer is idle.
  • Hola confirmed the supply chain compromise and said about 0.1% of its users were affected, with no evidence of user data access, theft, or compromise.

Supreme Court rules FCC fines punishing telecom giants for sharing location data were legal — Should Read

The Supreme Court ruled 8-1 that the FCC legally imposed nearly $200 million in fines on major telecom companies for sharing customers’ location data without proper consent. The decision rejects AT&T and Verizon’s claim that the agency’s fine process violated their right to a jury trial.

  • The Supreme Court ruled in an 8-1 decision that the FCC acted legally when it fined telecom companies for sharing access to consumers’ location data without their consent.
  • The Court held that the fines against AT&T and Verizon did not violate the companies’ rights to a jury trial, despite their argument that the FCC’s standalone fine process is unconstitutional.
  • The FCC fined Verizon nearly $47 million, AT&T more than $57 million, and T-Mobile and Sprint $92 million over the location data practices.
  • The FCC said the carriers failed to obtain consent and did not take reasonable measures to protect consumers’ location information before selling it to aggregators and third-party data brokers.
  • The FCC fines followed an investigation by Sen. Ron Wyden showing that a government contractor bought the data and created a self-service website used by law enforcement to obtain phone location data without a court order.

CISA directive for AI executive order to be released this week, Andersen says — Should Read

CISA plans to issue a binding directive this week telling federal agencies how to implement the new AI executive order, with a focus on vulnerability management and using AI to improve cyber defense. The agency will also help build a cyber clearinghouse and gain access to AI models for vetting.

  • CISA plans to release a binding operational directive by the end of the week requiring federal agencies to take actions to implement the president’s artificial intelligence executive order.
  • CISA Acting Director Nick Andersen said the directive will focus in part on vulnerability alleviation and vulnerability management.
  • Andersen said CISA will provide specific artificial intelligence access to partners in the coming days.
  • The artificial intelligence executive order asks companies to voluntarily submit models to the government for testing 30 days before public release.
  • Andersen said CISA will help establish the cyber clearinghouse envisioned by the executive order and will access models to vet.

Microsoft blames unexpected Windows driver updates on caching issue — Should Read

Microsoft says a Windows Update caching misconfiguration caused some Intune-managed Windows devices to ignore driver auto-update blocking policies and install Microsoft-signed drivers unexpectedly. The issue has been fixed, but some admins reported large-scale BIOS and driver updates that disrupted audio or video devices.

  • Microsoft fixed an issue that caused some Windows devices to install driver updates without notice despite policies configured to prevent automatic updates.
  • Microsoft attributed the problem to a misconfiguration in the Windows Update caching service that temporarily dropped device enrollment information and caused affected devices to be treated as non-enrolled.
  • Microsoft said the drivers installed during the incident were Microsoft approved and signed and did not pose a security threat.
  • Microsoft updated the affected service cache and the enrollment status for affected devices, then confirmed the issue was resolved after validation with a subset of previously affected users.
  • Windows administrators reported that tens of thousands of devices unexpectedly received BIOS and driver updates, with some cases causing audio or video devices to stop functioning.

Trump considers Palantir exec to lead CISA — May Read

The Trump administration is reportedly considering Palantir CTO Shyam Sankar to lead CISA, though the White House disputed that the selection is accurate at this time. The role has been vacant since January 2025 as CISA faces major AI-related responsibilities under a new executive order.

  • The Trump administration is considering Palantir Technologies Chief Technology Officer Shyam Sankar to lead the Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency, according to two sources familiar with the matter.
  • A White House official disputed the potential selection after publication, saying, "at this time this is not accurate."
  • CISA has not had a Senate-confirmed director since Jen Easterly stepped down in January 2025, and Acting Director Nick Andersen has led the agency since February.
  • Department of Homeland Security Secretary Markwayne Mullin told lawmakers that the administration is close to nominating a CISA director.
  • CISA was named in the administration’s new artificial intelligence executive order as one of the key agencies responsible for implementing the president’s vision.

Russia seeks to label two anti-Kremlin hacker groups as ‘extremist’ — May Read

Russia is moving to label the anti-Kremlin hacking groups Belarusian Cyber Partisans and Silent Crow as extremist organizations, which would effectively ban their activities in the country. The groups have claimed major cyberattacks on Russian and Belarusian targets, including a disruptive 2025 Aeroflot breach.

  • Russia’s Supreme Court will consider a request in a closed-door hearing to designate Belarusian Cyber Partisans and Silent Crow as extremist organizations and ban their activities in Russia.
  • Belarusian Cyber Partisans and Silent Crow have previously claimed responsibility for cyberattacks targeting critical infrastructure and government institutions in Russia and Belarus.
  • The two groups claimed responsibility for a July 2025 cyberattack on Aeroflot that forced the airline to cancel more than 100 flights and disrupted travel for roughly 20,000 passengers.
  • The hackers claimed they destroyed Aeroflot’s IT infrastructure and exfiltrated sensitive data, including flight records, internal call recordings, and information from employee monitoring systems.
  • Belarusian Cyber Partisans emerged after mass protests in Belarus in 2020 and has carried out cyber operations against Belarusian state institutions and the country’s railway network.

FTC considers setting aside or modifying $150 million privacy penalty against X — May Read

The FTC is considering whether to modify or set aside its 2022 order and $150 million penalty against Twitter, now X, over the use of account security data for targeted advertising. X argues the order is outdated and burdensome, while the FTC is seeking public comment before deciding.

  • The Federal Trade Commission is considering modifying or setting aside a 2022 settlement order with Twitter, now X, over the use of account security data for targeted advertising.
  • Twitter was fined $150 million in 2022 and prohibited from continuing to profit from data that the FTC said had been deceptively collected.
  • X petitioned the FTC, arguing that the order is unfair because it was issued against a company that no longer exists, the workers responsible no longer work for X, and the company has established a privacy and data protection program.
  • The FTC has invited public comment on whether it should change or set aside the order, with the comment period ending July 2.
  • The FTC alleged in 2022 that Twitter encouraged users to provide phone numbers and email addresses for account security but also used that data to target ads to specific consumers.

Police dismantles fake ID marketplace used by migrant smugglers — May Read

French and Spanish authorities dismantled an online marketplace allegedly supplying fake European identity documents to migrant smuggling networks, arresting a suspect in Alicante and seizing production equipment and about 800 counterfeit IDs. Europol said the operation highlights how document fraud supports migrant smuggling and other criminal activity across the EU.

  • French and Spanish authorities dismantled an online marketplace selling fake identity documents to migrant smuggling rings operating within the European Union.
  • On May 27, law enforcement arrested one suspect in Alicante, Spain, and seized document-production equipment and approximately 800 counterfeit European identity documents from an apartment rented under a false name.
  • The investigation began after French authorities identified a website advertising counterfeit identity documents and traced the suspect to Alicante, where he had lived since 2024.
  • Europol said the platform allegedly supplied forged identity and administrative documents in physical and digital formats to customers across Europe.
  • Europol said document fraud is a major enabler of fraudulent residency legalization and migrant smuggling within the EU.

Ghostwriter v7: Safer Tokens, Scoped Access, and Better Automation — May Read

Ghostwriter v7 replaces user-managed JWT API tokens with safer opaque tokens and adds scoped service tokens for non-human automation. The key takeaway is that integrations, oplog utilities, and LLM-driven workflows can now run with narrower, auditable permissions instead of broad user-like access.

  • Ghostwriter v7.0.0 replaces user-managed JWT API tokens with opaque Ghostwriter Access Tokens identified by the gwat_ prefix and backed by hashed secrets.
  • Ghostwriter v7 introduces service principals and scoped Ghostwriter Service Tokens identified by the gwst_ prefix for non-human automation.
  • Service tokens have permissions assigned directly to the token rather than inherited from the creating user, enabling narrower access for integrations.
  • Ghostwriter v7 adds operation log read and write tokens scoped to a single operation log and project read-only tokens scoped to selected projects or all projects the creator can access.
  • Users upgrading from earlier versions must rotate existing tokens to receive new opaque credentials and are advised to review whether some token use cases should switch to service tokens.

Brave Software releases Origin for a paid, bloat-free browsing experience — May Read

Brave Software launched Brave Origin, a paid $59.99 version of its browser that removes crypto, AI, rewards, promotions, and other monetization features while keeping Brave’s privacy protections. The release has drawn criticism from users who say Brave is charging for a cleaner experience that many believe should already be the default.

  • Brave Software announced the public release of Brave Origin, a paid minimalist version of its browser that removes cryptocurrency, AI, rewards, and other monetization-focused features.
  • Brave Origin disables features including Brave Rewards, Brave Wallet, Brave VPN promotions, Brave Leo AI, Brave News, Brave Talk, sponsored images, and other promotional or monetization components.
  • Brave Origin still includes Brave Shields, the browser's built-in privacy and ad-blocking protections.
  • Brave Origin is available as a standalone browser download and as an upgrade option for existing Brave installations, with a one-time $59.99 license for up to 10 devices and a free Linux version.
  • Some users criticized Brave Origin as charging users to remove unwanted features, while others noted that many of those features can already be disabled in the free Brave version using enterprise group policies.

OMB Resets Federal Logging. Now What? — Optional

OMB’s new federal logging guidance shifts agencies from collecting as much telemetry as possible to prioritizing actionable, risk-based visibility that supports real-time detection, investigation, and response. The key takeaway is that effective security now depends on useful, contextual data—not log volume.

  • OMB M-26-14 shifts federal logging guidance away from maximizing log volume and toward collecting telemetry that provides operational value.
  • The guidance directs agencies to use a risk-based, prioritized approach to logging that supports continuous event monitoring and threat hunting, investigation, response, and forensics.
  • The article says prior logging models created excessive data volumes, rising SIEM storage and ingestion costs, analyst noise, and reduced operational clarity.
  • Agencies are expected to maintain effective visibility across cloud, SaaS, OT, IoT, and hybrid environments, not rely on logging alone.
  • Netskope says its platform supports the guidance by providing real-time visibility across cloud, SaaS, web, AI, and private applications with context such as identity, device posture, location, and behavior.

Hackers Are After the Gaps in Your Vulnerability Program: Here's Their Playbook — Optional

Underground forums are teaching novice hackers a simple workflow to find, validate, and monetize exposed vulnerabilities using public tools, lowering the barrier to real-world exploitation. Defenders should prioritize reachable critical flaws, neglected legacy systems, and effective vulnerability disclosure programs before attackers act.

  • A forum post by a threat actor using the name "Hercules" explains how to search for newly disclosed vulnerabilities, identify exposed systems, validate whether they may be vulnerable, and decide whether to report, sell, or exploit the findings.
  • Flare researchers found that the thread drew sustained engagement from users who thanked Hercules, requested private contact or mentorship, described themselves as beginners, and sought guidance on applying hacking knowledge in practice.
  • The tutorial highlights the Nuclei framework, discusses defender challenges in patching newly disclosed vulnerabilities, and separates its workflow into legal and illegal paths.
  • Hercules describes monetization options including asking affected owners or hosting companies for payment, selling findings on underground markets, or exploiting vulnerabilities to inspect what is on a server.
  • The article says defenders should focus on critical reachable vulnerabilities, the long tail of old vulnerabilities on legacy systems, and paid vulnerability disclosure programs.

Tenable joins Anthropic’s Project Glasswing to advance AI-era cyber defense — Optional

Tenable is joining Anthropic’s Project Glasswing to test how Claude Mythos Preview can improve exposure management tasks like attack path analysis, risk prioritization, and remediation. The partnership is also aimed at helping Tenable and its customers better understand and secure the growing AI attack surface.

  • Tenable is participating in Anthropic’s Project Glasswing and working with Claude Mythos Preview to evaluate how frontier AI reasoning capabilities can improve exposure management programs.
  • Tenable plans to assess Claude Mythos Preview for attack path analysis, exposure prioritization, remediation decision-making, and strengthening Tenable’s own security.
  • The article says Tenable previously integrated the Tenable One Exposure Management Platform with the Claude Compliance API to provide AI visibility and governance capabilities.
  • Tenable expects frontier AI models to become an important source of security insight and telemetry for exposure management platforms.
  • Tenable says organizations increasingly need controls for AI systems they did not build as AI adoption accelerates.

Make Your Security Tools Smarter with Internet Intelligence — Optional

Censys argues that SOC teams can triage alerts faster and more accurately by enriching IOCs with current and historical Internet intelligence directly inside SIEM, SOAR, TIP, and investigation tools. Integrations with platforms like ThreatQ, Maltego, and Vertex Synapse help analysts validate indicators, find related infrastructure, and prioritize risk without constant tool-switching.

  • Security teams often have enough investigation data and tools, but they struggle to add sufficient context to alerts without forcing analysts to switch between multiple consoles.
  • Censys Internet intelligence can show how external assets look now and how they appeared historically, including at the time an incident occurred.
  • Censys data can be used inside SIEM, SOAR, threat intelligence, and investigative platforms to speed triage, enrich investigations, and identify related infrastructure.
  • Maltego can ingest Censys attributes such as network ownership, geolocation, HTTP headers, DNS relationships, certificate data, open ports, JARM and JA4 signatures, labels, and threats to help analysts find related assets.
  • Vertex Synapse can store Censys scanning observations, correlate them across investigations, and help analysts track the evolution of malicious or otherwise interesting infrastructure.

Identify in the SOC: Why Network Visibility Still Matters | Corelight — Optional

Corelight argues that SOC investigations still need strong network visibility enriched with identity provider context, because neither NDR nor EDR alone can fully answer who is behind suspicious activity. The key takeaway is that identity data and network telemetry should be combined to make threat investigations faster and more accurate.

  • The article says usernames are no longer sufficient to secure a network.
  • The article says SOC analysts investigating threats need to answer questions such as who a user is and why they are logging into a server.
  • The article states that neither Network Detection and Response nor Endpoint Detection and Response can directly answer the identity-related question posed during an investigation.
  • The article introduces the Identity Provider as relevant to answering identity questions in SOC investigations.

Agentic AI Is Transforming Defense, But Only Secure IT Infrastructure Will Maximize It — Optional

Agentic AI could give U.S. defense and intelligence agencies major decision-making advantages, but it also creates serious risks if deployed without secure infrastructure. The key takeaway is that classified AI systems need strong access controls, data governance, cross-domain protections, and hardware-enforced security built in from the start.

  • The article says advanced AI can help accelerate decision superiority for American forces as the U.S. government moves to deploy AI capabilities on classified networks.
  • The article argues that AI adoption requires security, governance, and resilient infrastructure around models, not just the deployment of powerful models.
  • The article identifies risks around what data enters AI models, who or what can access them, and where AI agents reach across databases, mission systems, or coalition partners.
  • The article states that sensitive data must move securely across classification boundaries, with threats and policy violations identified before they reach a model.
  • The article says trusted infrastructure, strict access controls, and strong data governance are mission critical for AI tools operating across domains, compartments, and operational theaters.

Winning the cyber marathon with Tony Giandomenico — Optional

Tony Giandomenico says rapid advances in AI are accelerating both attackers and defenders, making human-led threat hunting and clear leadership more important than ever. Cisco Talos is expanding its threat hunting capabilities beyond endpoint telemetry to include Secure Firewall and identity products like Duo and Cisco Identity Intelligence.

  • Tony Giandomenico said the biggest cybersecurity shift in the past six months has been the increasing capabilities of frontier AI models.
  • Giandomenico said AI will supercharge both adversaries moving through networks and defenders using the same technology to speed up security work.
  • Cisco Talos Threat Hunting looks for threats that evade existing security alerts and detection mechanisms by combining AI with human-in-the-loop services.
  • Cisco Talos is expanding threat hunting from Secure Endpoint telemetry to Secure Firewall and identity products including Duo and Cisco Identity Intelligence.
  • Giandomenico said leading major launches requires influencing people across departments by helping them understand and believe in the vision.

Live@eXchange Day 2 – Health-ISAC Medical Device Security Analyst — Optional

Health-ISAC’s Taylor Porter discussed medical device security at AAMI eXchange, highlighting Health-ISAC and AAMI’s annual threat report work with healthcare delivery organizations and device manufacturers. Key concerns included recent malicious actor takedowns affecting healthcare, phishing-as-a-service, and remote workforce hiring threats.

  • Health-ISAC’s Medical Device Security Analyst Taylor Porter was interviewed at AAMI eXchange in Denver.
  • The interview discussed Health-ISAC and AAMI’s partnership to conduct an annual threat report with Health Delivery Organizations and Medical Device Manufacturers.
  • The interview covered an HTM Week webinar about the annual survey results, which is available on the AAMI website.
  • The interview addressed recent malicious actor takedowns affecting the health sector, phishing as a service, and remote workforce hiring threats.
  • The article links to a YouTube video of the interview and notes that the Health-ISAC segment begins at minute 49:50.
entry.summary: This 24-hour digest is dominated by supply-chain compromise, exposed payment and personal data, public exploit availability, and the continued operationalization of AI across both attack and defense. Several reports also show attackers abusing trusted platforms—Stripe, Google Tag Manager, npm,...

entry.title: TA4922 Atlas RAT, ATG Targeting, HTTP/2 Bomb, and Nobitex Sanctions
entry.content: This coverage window is dominated by reporting on expanding intrusion activity, infrastructure-facing disruption risks, and government action against cyber-enabled financial networks. The most operationally relevant items include TA4922’s use of new malware families in Europe, attacks against internet-exposed fuel tank monitoring systems, and a newly disclosed HTTP/2 denial-of-service technique affecting major web servers. The reporting also highlights continuing security challenges at the intersection of AI assistants and user messaging workflows, alongside U.S. policy moves targeting cryptocurrency infrastructure allegedly tied to Iranian sanctions evasion and ransomware-linked actors. Proofpoint reported that the Chinese-speaking cybercrime group TA4922 has expanded beyond East Asian targeting to organizations in Germany, Italy, the United Kingdom, and South Africa. The group is using localized phishing themes that impersonate payroll notices, tax audits, VAT filings, government compliance messages, invoices, and HR communications, and has also attempted engagement through WhatsApp, LINE, and Microsoft Teams. A central development is the use of Atlas RAT, a newly identified remote access trojan with capabilities for reconnaissance, file theft, payload download, keylogging, screenshot capture, audio and webcam recording, and system shutdown or reboot. Proofpoint also identified RomulusLoader, which can execute additional payloads through process hollowing, shellcode injection, and direct execution, including legitimate remote management tools such as AnyDesk and SyncFuture. The same reporting described SilentRunLoader, a Python-based loader and information stealer deployed against organizations in the United Kingdom and Southeast Asia. SilentRunLoader was observed targeting Google Chrome credentials, cookies, and browsing data, adding credential theft and browser data collection to TA4922’s broader intrusion toolkit. CISA, the FBI, the NSA, the Department of Energy, and other U.S. government partners warned that attackers are targeting internet-exposed automatic tank gauge systems used to monitor fuel and liquid storage tanks. The agencies described compromises involving authentication bypass, hardcoded credentials, operating system command-execution flaws, SQL injection, and privilege-escalation weaknesses. Compromise of automatic tank gauge systems could allow attackers to alter network settings, product identifiers, tank volumes, pump controls, and alerts, potentially affecting operators’ visibility into tank fill levels. The advisory did not attribute the activity to a specific actor, though CNN previously reported suspected Iranian involvement in similar breaches affecting gas station tank monitoring systems in multiple U.S. states. Separately, researchers at Calif disclosed “HTTP/2 Bomb,” a denial-of-service technique combining HPACK compression amplification with Slowloris-style resource retention through HTTP/2 flow-control stalling. Testing showed rapid memory exhaustion across common server technologies, including Envoy 1.37.2, Apache httpd 2.4.67, nginx 1.29.7, and IIS on Windows Server 2025, with fixes reported for nginx 1.29.8 and Apache httpd modhttp2 2.0.41 at the time of publication. SafeBreach researcher Or Yair disclosed that poisoned notifications from messaging and social applications such as WhatsApp, Slack, SMS, Signal, Instagram, or Messenger could hijack Google Gemini on Android through indirect prompt injection. The issue affected Gemini’s Android Utilities feature, which can read and reply to notifications, and did not require the victim to install a malicious application. The research described a bypass technique called Fake Context Alignment, which made a user’s spoken “Yes” appear to authorize a sensitive action while the user heard a benign interaction. Demonstrated impacts included smart home control through Google Home, opening URLs, forcing the phone into a Zoom call, poisoning Gemini’s long-term account-level memory, and creating persistent scheduled actions. Google confirmed on November 14, 2025, that server-side content-classifier improvements mitigated the notification injection and Delayed Tool Invocation bypass. The U.S. Treasury’s Office of Foreign Assets Control sanctioned Nobitex, Iran’s largest cryptocurrency exchange, citing facilitation of payments related to terrorist activity, sanctions evasion, the IRGC, and IRGC-affiliated ransomware actors. Treasury said Nobitex processed more than half of all Iranian digital asset inflows in 2025, while Chainalysis reported that Iran’s cryptocurrency ecosystem received nearly $7.8 billion that year and that IRGC-associated addresses accounted for more than 50% of value received in Q4 2025. OFAC also designated Nobitex executives and founders, including chairman Amir Hossein Rad, CEO Seyed Ali Khoee, co-founder Seyed Mohammad Ali Aghamir Mohammad Ali, and blockchain lead Seyed Mohammad Aghamir Mohammad Ali. The broader U.S. “Economic Fury” campaign also targeted Iranian exchanges Wallex, Bitpin, and Ramzinex. In U.S. cyber policy reporting, Homeland Security Secretary Markwayne Mullin signaled efforts to reshape CISA after workforce losses and budget reductions during the second Trump administration. Reporting from The Record noted bipartisan concern over proposed fiscal 2027 cuts exceeding $700 million, CISA staffing levels of roughly 2,200 personnel despite authority for up to 3,400, and the absence of a Senate-confirmed CISA director since Trump returned to office.
entry.summary: This coverage window is dominated by reporting on expanding intrusion activity, infrastructure-facing disruption risks, and government action against cyber-enabled financial networks. The most operationally relevant items include TA4922’s use of new malware families in Europe, attacks against...
